accessibility_considerations: Action sheet and dialog are platform-native or styled per platform — both inherit good a11y. Custom edge pan is purely additive; VoiceOver users still have the navigation back button. Dialog announces title automatically on open.
performance_notes: BackHandler subscription is cheap and managed in useEffect cleanup. Edge pan gesture is only enabled when dirty=true (saves gesture handler overhead when not needed). ActionSheet is native — zero JS work. Dialog Modal mounts only when visible.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Respecting BOTH platforms' back conventions in one hook — iOS edge swipe and Android back button. 2) Confirmation UI per platform: action sheet vs Material dialog. 3) gestureEnabled toggle on Expo Router stack means we cooperate with native nav, not fight it.

title: Glassmorphism settings panel using native iOS blur (and Android-acceptable fallback)
design_brief: A settings overlay panel inspired by iOS 17 Control Center and Apple Music Now Playing: a card with a heavy native blur background, subtle vibrancy effect tinting the blur from underlying content, fine 1.5px stroke outline, and content (toggles, sliders, buttons) floating clearly above. On Android, where real-time backdrop blur is expensive on most devices, fall back to a deeply translucent dark surface with a subtle inner glow. Reference: iOS Control Center, Apple Music Now Playing, macOS Spotlight.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: BlurView on Android with intensity > 30 (kills GPU on mid-tier)
Hardcoded background color when iOS has real blur available
Pretending Android blur looks like iOS blur (it doesn't, and shouldn't)
Stacking multiple BlurViews (each is a render pass)
Using the deprecated react-native-blur — use expo-blur
mobile_reasoning: iOS UIVisualEffectView is GPU-accelerated and free at runtime. Android backdrop blur via expo-blur is implemented but expensive — it works on Pixel devices but stutters on $200 phones. The honest answer is: USE native blur on iOS, use a stylized translucent panel on Android. Don't try to make them look identical. Vibrancy (text/icon tinting based on underlying content) is iOS-only via the tint prop. The 1.5px stroke outline gives the glass a 'physical edge' even where the blur is subtle.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: GlassPanel component branches on Platform.OS for the backdrop layer. Inner content layer identical across platforms.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: const GLASS = {radius: 24, strokeWidth: 1.5, iosBlurIntensity: 70, iosTint: 'dark' as const, androidBg: 'rgba(28, 28, 32, 0.88)'};
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: This task IS the platform difference. iOS: BlurView with vibrancy. Android: solid translucent View + subtle inner shadow.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Panel max-width 480 centered. Edge-to-edge on small phones.

animation_strategy: Panel entrance: scale 0.92 → 1 spring + opacity 0 → 1 timing 240ms. Inner toggles use standard switches.
gesture_strategy: Tap outside dismisses (Pressable scrim). Pan-down on panel header dismisses if velocity > threshold.
interaction_details: Open: scale 0.92→1 spring + fade in 240ms
Pan down: panel translates with finger, drag past 80px or fast flick dismisses
Tap outside: scrim Pressable dismisses with fade
iOS: backdrop blurs underlying content — content tints through the blur subtly
Android: backdrop is opaque-ish dark; users don't expect blur magic on $200 phones
accessibility_considerations: Wrap content in View with accessibilityViewIsModal=true so VoiceOver doesn't leak to underlying screen. Scrim Pressable has accessibilityRole='button' accessibilityLabel='Close panel'. Reduce Transparency setting on iOS: AccessibilityInfo.isReduceTransparencyEnabled() → swap BlurView for the solid Android-style backdrop (Apple's HIG explicitly recommends this).
performance_notes: iOS BlurView is GPU-cheap. Android solid backdrop is even cheaper. Stroke is a single overlay View (no border-on-blur which composites poorly). Animations are all worklet-driven. The whole panel only mounts when visible=true.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Real iOS UIVisualEffectView via expo-blur — visually identical to Control Center. 2) Honest fallback on Android instead of fake blur — respects the platform's strengths. 3) Reduce Transparency awareness — iOS users with the setting on get the accessible variant automatically.

title: Sectioned FlashList with sticky headers and per-section live counts
design_brief: A contacts-app-style sectioned list (Apple Contacts, WhatsApp): items grouped alphabetically with sticky headers that scroll up under the next header. A right-edge scroll bar lets the user jump to a section by dragging. Each section header shows the live count of items in that section. Reference: Apple Contacts, WhatsApp Chats, Apple Music Library.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: SectionList (slower than FlashList, no recycling)
Re-computing section data on every render
Header count from data.filter on every render (re-iterates whole array)
Right-edge scroll bar as separate scrollable list (memory waste)
Hardcoded section header height (FlashList tolerates variance but needs estimate)
mobile_reasoning: FlashList supports stickyHeaderIndices natively in v2 — but data must be a flat array with mixed types (header + item). We precompute the sectioned flat array + indices once via useMemo. Section counts are folded into the header data at compute time — no per-render filtering. The right-edge index scrubber is a single absolutely-positioned column that maps drag-y to section index, calls scrollToIndex on FlashList. Sectioning is data structure work, not React work.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: Sectionizer utility produces {flat, indices, sectionOffsets}. FlashList consumes flat. IndexScrubber is sibling.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: None — FlashList works identically.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Index scrubber compact on small screens (single-letter A-Z), expanded on tablets.

animation_strategy: Scrubber drag: useSharedValue tracks finger y, derives section index, calls runOnJS(scrollToIndex). Optional: small bubble showing current letter follows finger.
gesture_strategy: Gesture.Pan on scrubber. activeOffsetY([-5, 5]) — should activate on small movement.
interaction_details: Scroll up: sticky letter headers slide under next header (FlashList native behavior)
Drag on right-edge scrubber: selection haptic on each new letter crossed, list jumps to that section
Header shows live count per section (folded into data at sectionize time)
Tap contact: navigates to detail
Smooth 60fps scrolling even with 5000+ contacts (FlashList recycling)
accessibility_considerations: Section headers have accessibilityRole='header'. Each contact row has accessibilityRole='button' with name. Scrubber has accessibilityRole='adjustable' with accessibilityActions increment/decrement for letter navigation without gesture. List has accessibilityLabel describing total count.
performance_notes: sectionize runs once per contacts change (useMemo). FlashList recycles header and item cells separately via getItemType. Sticky indices are precomputed — no per-render computation. Scrubber drag uses worklet to compute letter; only calls runOnJS when letter changes (not per pixel).
what_makes_this_native: 1) Right-edge index scrubber with haptic per letter — pure mobile pattern from Apple Contacts. 2) FlashList sticky headers behave like native UITableView sticky headers, including the 'slide under' effect. 3) Selection haptic per section gives audible-feeling feedback during fast scrubbing.

title: Pinch-to-zoom photo viewer with pan, double-tap-to-zoom, and bounded edges
design_brief: A full-screen photo viewer (Apple Photos, Instagram zoom): pinch with two fingers to zoom in to 5x max, pan when zoomed in to navigate around the image, double-tap to zoom to 2x at the tap point (or back to 1x if already zoomed), rubber-band when panning past image bounds, snap back to fit on release. Reference: Apple Photos, Instagram photo zoom, Telegram media viewer.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using a library like react-native-image-zoom-viewer (legacy patterns)
Allowing pan when at scale 1 (should pass to parent for next-photo swipe)
Linear zoom with no rubber-band at max
Pan without bounding to image edges
Resetting transform without spring physics
mobile_reasoning: Pinch + Pan composed with Gesture.Simultaneous. Pan is disabled (or rather, fails) when scale === 1 so the parent ViewPager handles next-photo swipe. The transform origin for pinch must be at the focal point of the two fingers (gesture.focalX/Y), otherwise zoom feels off-center. Bounded pan: when zoomed, the allowed translation = (image_w * scale - screen_w) / 2 on each axis. Past those bounds, rubber-band with diminishing return.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: PhotoViewer component owns gestures. Image fills container.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: None — gestures behave identically.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Image sizing: contentFit='contain' lets image use available space without cropping.

animation_strategy: scale, translateX, translateY shared values. On end: clamp scale to [1, 5] with spring. Clamp translation to bounds with spring. Double-tap: scale snaps to target with spring + translation adjusts to keep tap point under finger.
gesture_strategy: Gesture.Pinch + Gesture.Pan composed simultaneously. Pan.enabled() reactive to scale > 1.05. Gesture.Tap.numberOfTaps(2) for double tap.
interaction_details: Pinch: zooms around the focal point of the two fingers
Single tap: passes through to onSingleTap (e.g., toggle controls overlay)
Double tap: selection haptic + zoom to 2x at tap point (or back to 1x)
Pan when zoomed: follows finger, rubber-bands past bounds (resistance 0.4)
Release: settles to clamped scale + position with spring
accessibility_considerations: Image has accessibilityLabel describing the photo. accessibilityActions=[{name:'zoomIn'}, {name:'zoomOut'}, {name:'magicTap', label:'Toggle controls'}] for users without gesture access. Reduce Motion: animations skip — zoom is instant.
performance_notes: All transforms on UI thread via shared values. Pinch/pan/tap composed via Gesture.Simultaneous so they cooperate without race conditions. Rubber-band math is cheap arithmetic in worklets. expo-image cache means the photo doesn't re-decode on transform.
what_makes_this_native: 1) True multi-touch pinch around focal point — web touch events can do this but rarely as smoothly. 2) Rubber-band at bounds is the iOS/Android system convention everyone expects. 3) Single tap vs double tap arbitration via requireExternalGestureToFail handles the 250ms window correctly.

title: Live tracking polyline that animates as new GPS points arrive
design_brief: A Strava/Citymapper live tracking screen: as user moves, GPS pings every 2s, polyline extends along the path with a smooth interpolation rather than a hard new vertex. Current position marker pulses. Map auto-follows the user (but a manual pan disables auto-follow until tap on 'recenter' button). Reference: Strava live tracking, Apple Maps navigation, Citymapper Go.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Calling setState on every GPS ping (re-renders whole map)
Hard-jumping polyline endpoints
No animation between GPS samples
Auto-follow that fights user pan
Listening to location with watchPositionAsync at 1Hz without distanceInterval
mobile_reasoning: GPS samples arrive sparse — interpolating linearly between samples over the 2s interval gives a smooth trail. Polyline coordinates are stored in a useRef array; map.setNativeProps updates coordinates without going through React reconciliation. User pan disables auto-follow via onPanDrag listener. Recenter button re-enables and animates back to user.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: LiveTrackMap component owns Location + MapView refs. Recenter FAB anchored bottom-right above safe area.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS: Location.requestForegroundPermissionsAsync. Android: same expo-location API. Background updates need additional setup not in this scope.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: FAB at right-16, bottom-safearea+24. Map fills screen.

animation_strategy: User marker pulse: useFrameCallback with sine wave on scale 1↔1.2. Polyline extension: useEffect on new sample interpolates 5 intermediate points and updates polyline ref over 2s.
gesture_strategy: Map's native pan triggers onPanDrag → disables auto-follow. Recenter tap re-enables.
interaction_details: GPS update every ~2s with smooth polyline extension
User position marker pulses softly (1 ↔ 1.25 scale, 900ms ease-in-out sine)
Pan map → recenter FAB appears with auto-follow off
Tap recenter → selection haptic + map animates back to user position
Polyline uses rounded caps + joins for organic feel
accessibility_considerations: Recenter button has accessibilityRole='button' and clear label. Map has accessibilityLabel describing tracking state. accessibilityLiveRegion='polite' on a hidden Text node could announce 'tracking active' / 'tracking paused'.
performance_notes: Polyline coordinates are an immutable array; react-native-maps diffs efficiently. Marker re-renders only on user position change (every 2s). Pulse animation is a single shared value with withRepeat on UI thread. No setState during pulse.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Native GPS via expo-location with distanceInterval — won't update for tiny noise jitter. 2) Map-native polyline rendering, not view-based — handles thousands of points without slowdown. 3) Pulse-on-position marker is a long-running animation that costs ~zero JS work via Reanimated.

title: Pull-to-refresh with custom spinner and progress-during-pull feedback
design_brief: A list pull-to-refresh that doesn't use the default RefreshControl. As user pulls, a custom indicator (e.g., growing arc) tracks the pull distance and rotates when ready to release. On release-past-threshold, the indicator becomes a full spinner; on data arrival, it morphs to a checkmark briefly before sliding away. Reference: Things 3 pull-to-refresh, Apple Mail, Twitter (RIP).
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Default <RefreshControl/> with no design intent
Indicator only animating ON release (should track during pull)
Hard release threshold with no visual signal
No success state — list just snaps back
Spinner that keeps spinning forever if data never arrives
mobile_reasoning: useAnimatedScrollHandler tracks overscroll (negative contentOffset.y on iOS). Custom indicator opacity/rotation derived from that overscroll value on UI thread. Release detection: onScrollEndDrag or a JS-side useEffect on a 'pullDistance > threshold' state. After release, indicator switches to spinning mode (rotation via withRepeat). On data arrival, runOnUI to morph to check + slide away. Timeout safety: if no data after 12s, show error and reset.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: PullToRefresh component wraps a scrollable child. Indicator sits absolutely above the scroll view.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS: overscroll bounce gives us free negative-scroll values. Android: requires overScrollMode='always' and may need a translateY workaround for the content.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Indicator centered horizontally, fixed top offset below safe area + nav bar.

animation_strategy: pullProgress derived from scrollY → [0..1]. Indicator: scale + opacity from 0 to 1 as progress grows. Past 1, rotation indicates 'ready'. On refresh start: rotation runs withRepeat. On complete: stop rotation, morph to check, fade out.
gesture_strategy: None custom — uses ScrollView's native bounce.
interaction_details: Pull begins: indicator fades in proportionally to pull distance
Pull crosses threshold: medium haptic, indicator at full scale
Release past threshold: refresh starts, indicator spins continuously
Data arrives: success haptic, brief solid arc, fades out over 280ms
Timeout (12s): error haptic, indicator vanishes — list isn't stuck
accessibility_considerations: accessibilityLiveRegion on a hidden text announcing 'Refreshing' / 'Refreshed' / 'Refresh failed'. accessibilityActions=[{name:'refresh'}] on the scroll container so VoiceOver users can trigger refresh from the rotor.
performance_notes: Indicator drawn via Skia — single canvas, single path. Rotation and pull progress all on UI thread. State machine in React only fires on transitions, not per frame.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Indicator tracks pull distance in real time on UI thread — feels like the indicator IS the gesture. 2) Haptic at threshold crossing tells the user 'release now' without looking. 3) Built-in timeout protects against the dreaded 'forever spinner' that web apps love to leave you with.

title: Wheel-style date picker with momentum scroll and haptic per row
design_brief: A Cron/Things-style native-feeling date picker: three vertical wheel columns (day, month, year) that the user spins with touch. Each row passing the center triggers a selection haptic. Snaps to nearest row on release with deceleration. Selected row visually highlighted with surrounding rows dimmed. Reference: iOS native UIPickerView, Cron calendar, Things 3 date picker.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Using @react-native-picker/picker (lots of platform quirks)
FlatList with snapToInterval but no haptic per row
Custom scroll that doesn't decelerate naturally
Haptics on every scroll frame (call once per item index change)
Hardcoded item height that breaks on dynamic type
mobile_reasoning: A Skia-free implementation uses Animated.ScrollView with snapToInterval = itemHeight, decelerationRate='fast'. The selected index is derived from scrollY: round(scrollY / itemHeight). useAnimatedReaction watches that derived index and fires haptic + onChange via runOnJS when it changes. The center is a fixed pill overlay. Top/bottom padding = (visibleRows / 2) * itemHeight so the first and last items can scroll to center.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: WheelColumn component (single column). DateWheel composes 3 columns + selection pill.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS: bounces enabled for soft edges. Android: overScrollMode='never' for tighter feel matching Material design.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Column widths flex-equal. itemHeight scales with PixelRatio.getFontScale() for accessibility.

animation_strategy: scrollY → derived index → useAnimatedReaction. Each visible row's opacity derived from |distance to center|.
gesture_strategy: ScrollView native vertical scroll. No custom gesture.
interaction_details: Spin: selection haptic per row passing center
Snap on release via snapToInterval + decelerationRate='fast'
Rows fade and shrink as they move away from center
Selection pill highlights the center row through all columns
No double-haptic on the same index (useAnimatedReaction tracks prev)
accessibility_considerations: Each column has accessibilityRole='adjustable' accessibilityLabel='Day picker'/'Month picker'/'Year picker' with accessibilityActions increment/decrement so VoiceOver users navigate via standard adjustable controls without scrolling.
performance_notes: Hooks are declared once at component top level; mapped marks are plain memoized data, so the Rules of Hooks are preserved.
what_makes_this_native: 1) Per-row haptic gives the wheel a 'detent' feel like an actual physical wheel. 2) Native snapToInterval + decelerationRate='fast' produces the iOS-spec wheel deceleration. 3) Opacity falloff for distant rows is the depth cue that makes the wheel feel 3D without 3D.

title: Sparkline list rows that animate on scroll into view
design_brief: A portfolio/watchlist list (Robinhood, Cash App investing): each row contains a small sparkline trend chart on the right. The sparklines animate-in (line draws from left to right over 400ms) the first time they scroll into view — subsequent appearances are instant. Reference: Robinhood watchlist, Apple Stocks, Cash App investing.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: Every sparkline re-animating on every render
SVG paths animated with strokeDashoffset on JS thread
Animating ALL sparklines on initial mount (visible ones first, lazy others)
Using ViewportObserver from web (not a thing on RN — use onViewableItemsChanged)
mobile_reasoning: FlashList exposes onViewableItemsChanged. We track a Set<string> of itemIds that have animated. When a new item enters viewable, mark it and let it animate. The sparkline itself is Skia — strokeDashoffset equivalent done by trimming the path. Animation is per-row UI-thread worklet — no shared state across rows.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: WatchlistRow with Sparkline subcomponent. Animated-once Set held in a ref at list level.
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: None — Skia abstracts.
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Sparkline width 80 on phone, 120 on tablet via useWindowDimensions.

animation_strategy: progress shared value 0→1 with withTiming(400ms). Drawn path uses trim — Skia Path.getTrimmed(0, progress).
gesture_strategy: Tap or focus a data mark to expose the exact value; the semantic legend mirrors the same interaction for screen readers.
interaction_details: First scroll into view: sparkline draws left to right over 400ms with ease-out
Recycled cells with previously-seen ids: render at progress=1 (no animation)
Up trend green, down trend red — color encoded
Tabular-num font variant keeps numeric columns aligned
accessibility_considerations: Each row has accessibilityRole='button' (assuming tappable) with accessibilityLabel='Symbol, Name, price $X.XX, change Y percent'. Sparkline has accessibilityElementsHidden because trend is in the label.
performance_notes: Sparkline path computed once per data change (useMemo). Animation only on first viewable — recycled rows skip it. onViewableItemsChanged fires sparsely (only when items cross threshold). Memoized Row prevents re-render unless item or firstSeen changes.
what_makes_this_native: 1) FlashList's viewable items API is RN-specific — no equivalent on the web outside libraries. 2) Skia path trim runs on UI thread — feels like a native draw animation. 3) Animate-once-per-id pattern keeps fast scrolling smooth (scrolling back to a row doesn't trigger another animation).

title: VoiceOver-friendly slider with custom design and accessibility actions
design_brief: A custom slider control (volume, brightness, settings) with a thicker thumb than the default, value pill that appears above on drag, and stepped increments. Must work seamlessly with VoiceOver — VoiceOver users can swipe up/down on the slider to increment/decrement, double-tap to activate. Reference: iOS Settings sliders, Apple Music volume, Android Material 3 slider.
anti_patterns_to_avoid: @react-native-community/slider with no accessibility customization
Slider that doesn't expose accessibilityValue (VoiceOver reads '50%' default)
Step values that don't snap visually
Value bubble that appears only on drag end (should appear during drag)
Touch target smaller than 44pt
mobile_reasoning: Custom sliders are an accessibility minefield. The pattern is: accessibilityRole='adjustable', accessibilityValue={{min, max, now, text: human-readable}}, onAccessibilityAction handles 'increment'/'decrement'/'activate'. VoiceOver users get the up/down swipe to step through values without any gesture work — these get routed to the same handlers as touch. The visual is irrelevant to VoiceOver; the contract is in those props.

stylesheet_strategy.organization: Slider component with track, fill, thumb, and value pill. Theme tokens for thumb size + colors.
stylesheet_strategy.constants: const SLIDER = {trackHeight: 6, thumbSize: 28, thumbActiveSize: 36, hitSlopV: 16, hitSlopH: 8};
stylesheet_strategy.platform_differences: iOS: shadow on thumb. Android: elevation. iOS thumb scales up on press (Apple convention); Android thumb stays same size (Material convention).
stylesheet_strategy.responsive_strategy: Track width fills container. Thumb size scales with text scale slightly for accessibility.

animation_strategy: thumbScale: useSharedValue → withSpring on press. valuePill opacity: useSharedValue → withTiming on drag start/end.
gesture_strategy: Pan gesture on the track. activeOffsetX([-3, 3]) so the slider activates with minimal movement. failOffsetY([-10, 10]) so the slider doesn't fight vertical list scroll.
interaction_details: Drag: selection haptic on each step crossed
iOS: thumb scales up to 1.25 on press (Apple HIG)
Android: thumb stays same size (Material 3)
Value pill fades in above thumb during drag, fades out on release
VoiceOver: swipe up/down on slider increments/decrements by step, same haptic
accessibility_considerations: accessibilityRole='adjustable' is THE most important attribute — it tells VoiceOver this is a slider. accessibilityActions=[{name:'increment'},{name:'decrement'}] wires the standard rotor actions. accessibilityValue.text provides a human-readable description ('50 percent' beats '50'). hitSlop gives 60pt+ vertical touch target.
performance_notes: Single Pan gesture, single shared progress value. React re-renders only when value changes (one per step). Thumb position and pill position derived from progress on UI thread.
what_makes_this_native: 1) accessibilityRole='adjustable' makes VoiceOver swipes work without any custom code. 2) Platform-aware thumb scaling honors HIG (iOS) vs Material (Android). 3) Haptic per step makes the slider feel like a click-stop dial.
